Elk||100: Rocky Mountain foothills transitioning to boreal mixedwood. WMU 100 sits on the eastern edge of the Rockies southwest of Calgary. Elevations range from roughly 1,100–2,000m. Expect a mix of open meadows, lodgepole pine, and spruce, with good elk habitat in the upper benches.|||Access is primarily via Highway 40 and secondary forestry roads. Most of the WMU is accessible by 2WD on main roads; backcountry areas require high-clearance or ATV. A popular unit — competition for this draw is real. Tags are limited and odds hover around the 10–15% range historically. Elk numbers are solid but hunting pressure is moderate. Early season bulls in velvet, late season rut hunting can be exceptional. The proximity to Calgary means you will share the landscape with hikers and other recreationists. Go early, go deep, and have your access scouted before the season.
Elk||101: Southern foothills parkland, WMU 101 spans rolling hills and agricultural edges west of High River and Nanton. Elevation is moderate — mostly 1,000–1,500m. A mosaic of aspen bluffs, grass parks, and lodgepole pine. One of the better elk WMUs in southern AB for sheer numbers.|||Highway 22 is your spine. Most access is via gravel range roads, and a 2WD truck gets you to most glassing knobs. This WMU has private land mixed throughout — know your boundaries before you go, and knock on doors. Elk here can be resident animals or transitional between the mountains and the plains. The rut is the best window. Odds for this draw are competitive but not brutal — one of the more accessible elk draws in the south.
Elk||102: Heavily forested WMU in the foothills southwest of Calgary, stretching toward the Sheep River corridor. Dense lodgepole and spruce with riparian willow along creek drainages. Terrain is moderate to steep in the western portions, flatter and more open eastward.|||Main access off Highway 546 and associated forestry roads. A 4x4 is recommended for most backcountry roads especially in wet conditions. Elk are present in good numbers. Hunting pressure is moderate — the timber density means you need to work hard for a shot opportunity. Call elk in during the rut or work the edges at first and last light. Success rates are reasonable for hunters who put in the effort.
Elk||103: WMU 103 covers the Highwood River drainage south of Longview, foothills country transitioning to open range. Expect open ridge glassing country mixed with dense timber pockets. Elevation 1,200–1,800m. This is classic southern Alberta elk country.|||Highway 541 and Forestry Trunk Road (HWY 940) provide main access. Most roads are gravel and passable with a 2WD in dry conditions. A high-clearance 4x4 opens up the back corners. Private land borders in the east — verify maps. Elk numbers are reliable here; bulls can be found on high ridges glassing into timber. The rut is the premium hunting window. This draw sees moderate competition and success rates are average to good.
Elk||104: WMU 104 sits in the Porcupine Hills, a unique landscape of rolling grass ridges, coulees, and aspen groves west of Claresholm. Lower elevation elk country — mostly 1,000–1,400m. Open terrain makes this a glassing-first hunt.|||Access via Range Road networks and local gravel roads. Significant private land throughout — do your homework before hunting here. This is not wilderness elk hunting; it is more of a walk-in or quiet farm truck operation in many areas. Elk here are often resident herds that winter on private and move onto Crown land seasonally. The draw is competitive and success rates can vary depending on elk movement and private land access. Build relationships with landowners.
Elk||105: Forestry and foothills WMU west of Pincher Creek near Waterton. Excellent elk country with mountain views. Mix of open slopes, aspen benches, and dense spruce. The southern Rockies influence means dramatic terrain and reliable elk populations.|||Highway 6 and Castle River Road are main corridors. Castle Provincial Park and adjacent Crown land provide the hunting area. Roads are mostly gravel; a 4x4 is recommended west of the highway. Wind is a constant factor in this WMU — plan your approach accordingly. Elk numbers are good and the scenery is unbeatable. This is a draw that dedicated southern Alberta elk hunters work toward. Competition is real; apply consistently.
Elk||200: Boreal transition and aspen parkland country northwest of Edmonton. WMU 200 covers rolling terrain with a mix of agricultural clearing and boreal bush. Elk here are often in smaller resident herds using the bush-field edge.|||Access via secondary highways and range roads. Significant private land — permission hunting is the norm in the agricultural fringe. Crown land parcels require navigation. Elk can be intercepted at field edges at dawn and dusk. The rut is less spectacular here than in mountain country but elk are vocal and responsive to calls. Draw odds in this region can be more favorable than foothills WMUs.
Elk||300: Boreal forest WMU in the Swan Hills region. Dense mixedwood — white spruce, aspen, and muskeg pockets. Terrain is relatively flat with significant wet areas. This is the northern boreal elk hunting experience.|||Access off Highway 33 and associated logging roads. A high-clearance truck or ATV is essential — many roads are reclaimed seismic lines or soft logging grades. Elk numbers in the Swan Hills are good but hunting is challenging due to timber density. Still-hunting and rattling during the rut in open timber or cutblocks is the primary tactic. Expect to cover significant ground. Bugs in early season can be intense; come prepared.
Elk||400: Foothills parkland WMU in central AB west of Red Deer. A productive mix of lodgepole, spruce, and open meadows. Elevation is moderate. One of the more productive mid-province elk WMUs.|||Forestry Trunk Road (HWY 752/756 corridor) provides primary access. Roads are generally good but 4x4 helps in wet fall conditions. This is working elk country — plan to spend time scouting cutblocks and wallows. Draw odds here are moderate and hunter success rates are reasonable for those who prepare.
Elk||500: North-central boreal elk country. Dense spruce-aspen mixedwood with muskeg. Relatively flat terrain with water everywhere in wet years. Elk populations are present but dispersed.|||Accessed primarily via logging and seismic roads off the main highway grid. ATV or high-clearance 4x4 mandatory. Plan for long drives into the bush on poor roads. The remoteness keeps pressure low — hunters who put in the scouting find elk. Biting insects can be severe in early season. This is a serious commitment draw.
Moose||100: Foothills and Rocky Mountain fringe country in southern AB. WMU 100 moose habitat centers on willow-choked creek drainages, beaver ponds, and the upper elevation spruce. Not traditional moose country but resident animals exist.|||Access via Highway 40 and forestry roads. 4x4 recommended for backcountry. Moose in this WMU are often found in the low boggy areas along stream corridors. Drawing a moose tag in the foothills is excellent — pressure is low. Calling during the rut (September-October) is the top tactic. Be prepared for a significant pack-out from remote areas.
Moose||200: Parkland and boreal transition country. This is more classic moose habitat — willows, aspen, wetlands. Moose numbers in the 200-series WMUs are generally good.|||Access via secondary highways and range roads. Mix of Crown and private land. Moose hunting here often involves working wetland and pond edges at dawn. A canoe or small boat can be a major asset for accessing remote ponds. Draw odds for moose in parkland WMUs are moderate. Success rates are good for prepared hunters.
Moose||300: Prime boreal moose habitat. WMU 300-series covers classic northern AB moose country — black spruce bogs, willow flats, beaver meadows. Moose densities here are among the highest in the province.|||Accessed via highway and logging road networks. A high-clearance truck or ATV is essential to reach quality habitat. Plan to get off the main roads. Rut hunting in mid-October is the most productive window — bulls are vocal and respond well to calling. Pack-out logistics are important to plan before you go; a quartered bull moose is a serious load. Guides or outfitters operate in parts of this region.
Moose||400: Central AB boreal and parkland moose habitat. WMU 400-series offers a mix of terrain from open agricultural edges to denser boreal bush. Moose are distributed throughout.|||Access is generally good off main highways with secondary roads reaching most areas. A truck with clearance handles most roads. Calling and glassing wetland edges is the primary tactic. Draw odds in central WMUs are moderate. Check for ATV/motorized restrictions in specific sub-zones before heading out.
Moose||500: Northern boreal moose country. Some of the best moose habitat in Alberta is in the 500-series WMUs — productive peatlands, dense willow, and spruce bogs. Moose densities can be very high.|||Remote access via logging roads and seismic lines. A 4x4 or ATV is essential. Plan for several days minimum — distances are long. This is wilderness moose hunting at its best. The rut calling window is spectacular up north. Be prepared for cold, wet weather and difficult pack-out terrain. A meat cooler or game cart are essential gear here.
Moose||600: Peace Country and northern parkland transition. WMU 600-series covers rolling agricultural land blending into boreal. Moose use the bush corridors between farmland.|||Access is generally good — highway and range road networks are extensive. Some of the easiest access moose hunting in the province. However, private land is significant throughout — get permission or know your Crown areas. Moose are often found on agricultural edges at dawn and dusk. A hunt here is more accessible than northern boreal draws but may require more creative navigation around private land.
Moose||700: Northwest AB boreal and foothills transition. Diverse terrain from river valleys to upland spruce. Good moose country with relatively low hunting pressure in some areas.|||Forestry and logging road access. High-clearance required for many areas. The Grande Prairie region has a mix of road quality — main forest roads are good, spur roads less so. Moose hunting here can be excellent; the north is underappreciated by southern hunters. Weather can deteriorate quickly in fall — be prepared for snow and cold.
Mule Deer||100: Southern foothills and coulees. WMU 100-series mule deer country offers classic prairie fringe glassing hunts — open ridges, coulees, and river breaks. Terrain is open and ideal for spotting bucks at distance.|||Highway 22 corridor and range roads provide access. A 2WD truck reaches most areas. Mule deer in the foothills tend to follow elevation — up high in summer, dropping into coulees and valleys in late fall. November rut hunting is spectacular. Bucks in this region can be legitimately large. Draw odds are competitive for this reason. Glass first, stalk second — a good rangefinder and shooting sticks are essential.
Mule Deer||200: Parkland and river valley mule deer habitat. WMU 200-series sits where the foothills meet the parkland. Deer use river breaks, coulees, and brushy hillsides. Terrain is moderate and accessible.|||Range roads and secondary highways cover most of this area. Some private land to navigate. Mule deer here are transition animals — not quite mountain deer, not quite prairie deer. They hold in the heavier bush during the day and feed on agricultural edges. An accessible and productive draw for a solid representative buck.
Mule Deer||300: Central parkland mule deer. WMU 300-series supports mule deer in coulees, river breaks, and aspen bluffs. Terrain is rolling parkland — mix of open and treed.|||Access is good. Range roads reach most areas. Some of the best November rut hunting in central AB can be found in this region as bucks chase does across open terrain. Competition for mule deer draws has increased — apply consistently. A 2WD truck is fine for most access.
Mule Deer||400: Prairie and coulée mule deer in the south and central plains. Classic prairie mule deer hunting — wide open glassing, coulée systems, and river breaks. Bucks live in the cuts and move onto the flats during the rut.|||Range roads and township roads provide a grid across most of this area. Private land is the main challenge — get permission or know your Crown. A great set of binoculars and a long-range rifle are core equipment. The rut in November can produce exceptional buck encounters as deer cover ground. Draw odds vary but this is popular country.
Mule Deer||500: Southern AB badlands and coulée country — some of the most dramatic mule deer terrain in the province. Hoodoos, river breaks, and open prairie. The Red Deer River valley corridor holds good bucks.|||Highway 36 and 41 plus range roads provide access. ATVs and trucks both work. The badlands terrain can be rough on footwear and ankles — good boots matter. Glass from high points and spot-stalk. Water access in dry years is a factor for deer movement. This is bucket-list Alberta mule deer country.
White-tailed Deer||100: Southern foothills whitetail habitat. WMU 100-series supports resident whitetail in riparian corridors, aspen bluffs, and agricultural edges in the foothills. These are mountain-influenced deer — heavier bodies, big necks during the rut.|||Access via Highway 22 and range roads. A 2WD handles most access. Whitetail here respond well to rut tactics — rattling and calling in November. Dense bush pockets hold bucks during the day. A tree stand on a well-used trail or food source can be very effective. Draw odds for special licence whitetail are competitive.
White-tailed Deer||200: Parkland whitetail country. This is classic central AB deer hunting — aspen bluffs, grain fields, and willow draws. Whitetail densities in the 200-series parkland WMUs can be very high.|||Access via secondary highways and range roads. Significant private land — knock on doors or know your Crown parcels. Tree stands over food sources and trail systems are the dominant tactic. The November rut is the prime window. Bucks in parkland country can grow very large antlers due to excellent nutrition.
White-tailed Deer||300: Central parkland and boreal fringe whitetail. WMU 300-series sits in some of the most productive whitetail country in Alberta. Agricultural land and bush corridors provide excellent habitat.|||Good road access throughout. Tree stands, still-hunting along edges, and rattling during the rut all produce results. This region sees consistent big-buck production. Draw odds for special licence here can be competitive but the opportunity is worth applying for.
White-tailed Deer||600: Peace Country whitetail hunting. The 600-series WMUs in the Peace region support excellent whitetail populations in agricultural parkland and boreal fringe. Some very large bucks come out of this region.|||Good highway and range road access. The Peace Country is underrated for trophy whitetail — less pressure than central AB and excellent habitat. Private land is significant but many farmers are accommodating to respectful hunters. Plan a longer trip to make the most of the travel. November rut is prime time.
White-tailed Deer||700: Northwest AB parkland and boreal fringe. WMU 700-series covers the Grande Prairie region — excellent agricultural whitetail habitat. Big, cold-weather bucks are common here.|||Highway 43 corridor and range roads. Good access overall. The Grande Prairie region produces trophy-calibre whitetail regularly. Hunting here is a mix of field-edge tree stand work and bush still-hunting. Apply for this draw consistently — the opportunity is excellent.
Pronghorn Antelope||100: Shortgrass prairie and mixed-grass prairie in southeast AB. This is core pronghorn country — wide open, flat to gently rolling, with minimal cover. Antelope live in big groups and visibility is extreme. Glass everything.|||Highway 3, 36, 41, and township roads provide a grid. A 2WD truck on dry roads handles most access. Antelope here are skittish and have exceptional eyesight — long-range shooting capability matters. Water sources in dry August can concentrate animals dramatically. Draw odds for pronghorn are generally better than elk or moose but still limited in quota. The rut in October can make bucks somewhat less cautious.
Pronghorn Antelope||200: Palliser Triangle and mixed-grass prairie. Classic antelope range extending across the southeast. Terrain is flat to rolling with coulée systems cutting through. Animals use the coulée heads for bedding.|||Range road grid access. Most of southeastern AB is private agricultural land — Crown land is limited and patchy. Landowner permission is essential for most antelope hunting here. A polite knock and a respectful approach goes a long way. Antelope densities in a good year are excellent. Come with a good rifle capable of 300+ yard shots and solid shooting sticks.
Pronghorn Antelope||300: Dry mixed-grass and sagebrush country in southern AB. More broken terrain with coulées and river valleys mixed into the open prairie. Antelope use these breaks for shade and escape.|||Highway and range road access. Private land dominates — get permission early in the season. Antelope populations here fluctuate with winter severity. In a good year this is exceptional hunting. In a tough winter year numbers can be down noticeably. Check with local biologists or FWD for current herd condition before making plans.
Wood Bison||800: WMU 800-series encompasses the Hay-Zama and Rainbow Lake areas of far northwest AB, near the only free-ranging wood bison population in Alberta outside national parks. Terrain is boreal wetlands, muskeg, and spruce-dominated lowlands. This is remote, demanding country.|||Access is via Highway 35 north to High Level, then forestry roads west. This is one of the most remote hunts in the province. A high-clearance 4x4 or ATV is essential and even then access can be severely limited by soft ground and wet conditions. Wood bison tags are extraordinarily rare — a once-in-a-lifetime draw for most hunters. Quotas are tiny and success rates reflect the difficulty of the terrain and the rarity of the tags. If you draw this, hire a local outfitter or guide with experience in the area. The logistics are not trivial. Pack-out of a wood bison is a major undertaking — 900kg animals require significant infrastructure.
Wood Bison||900: WMU 900-series covers portions of the Cold Lake Air Weapons Range and adjacent boreal forest in east-central AB. The terrain is flat boreal forest — jack pine, black spruce, and muskeg. Wood bison in this region are a managed population.|||Access and hunting season logistics are subject to special permit conditions and potentially DND coordination for areas adjacent to the weapons range. Confirm all access restrictions with Alberta Fish and Wildlife before planning. These are extremely rare tags. The terrain is flat but remote — extensive logging and seismic road networks exist but condition varies. A 4x4 and ATV are recommended. This is a true wilderness experience and should be approached as such.
Bighorn Sheep||100: Rocky Mountain front ranges in southern AB — the core Alberta bighorn sheep range. WMU 100-series for sheep covers dramatic mountain terrain: sheer cliff faces, talus slopes, alpine meadows, and steep canyon walls. Expect elevations of 1,500–3,000m. Sheep live where it is steep.|||Access via Highway 40, 541, and Castle trails. Horses or significant backpacking are required to reach legal rams in most units. A sheep hunt in AB is one of the most physically demanding legal hunts in North America. Plan for 7–14 days on the mountain. A ram tag in AB is the pinnacle of big game draws — odds are in the single digits or lower. Most hunters apply for a decade or more before drawing. The country is stunningly beautiful and brutally unforgiving. Be in excellent physical condition and go in self-sufficient.
Bighorn Sheep||102: Southern foothills and front range sheep country south of Highway 541. Steep, rocky terrain with good grass on the high benches. Winter-range and summer-range sheep movements can be dramatic.|||Access from Castle area roads and backcountry trails. A horse is a significant advantage. Rams use cliff faces and open slides where predators cannot easily approach. Glassing is the primary technique — expect to cover 15–20 km per day on foot looking for legal rams. This draw is extremely coveted. Be prepared for weather — early September can be warm; late October can be full winter.
Bighorn Sheep||200: Mountain and foothills sheep country in central AB, including the Brazeau and Clearwater drainages. Terrain is alpine and subalpine — rugged and remote. Some of the biggest rams in AB come from central mountain country.|||Access via Forestry Trunk Road and backcountry trails. Horses or extended backpack trips required. This is serious backcountry — plan a full expedition. Weather in September and October can include significant snowfall. The reward for drawing a sheep tag here is extraordinary.
Mountain Goat||100: Extreme alpine terrain in the southern AB Rockies. Mountain goats inhabit the most vertical terrain available — cliff bands, glacial cirques, and exposed rocky ridges. Expect routes involving hands-and-feet scrambling. Elevations 2,000–3,000m.|||Access via southern foothills highways and trail systems. A goat hunt in AB is a technical undertaking — not just in shooting, but in getting to where goats live and getting the animal back out. Goats often don't run from hunters; the challenge is the terrain. Draw odds are very low and tags are rare. Physical fitness and backcountry mountain skills are not optional. Hire a guide if you have any doubt about self-sufficiency in technical terrain.
Mountain Goat||200: Central Rocky Mountain goat country. The Brazeau and Clearwater regions support good goat populations on the high rocky faces. Access is via the same backcountry routes used by sheep hunters.|||Multi-day backcountry expedition required. Horses or backpacking. Goat meat is excellent and the trophy value is high. These tags are rare. Once you have a goat down, the real work begins — goats tend to die in terrible places and the pack-out is notoriously difficult. Plan accordingly and do not underestimate the task.
